title: Automated Log Review — Correlation of Firewall Deny Bursts with IDS Signatures

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 11.4.1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 11.4.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.69: Automated Log Review — Correlation of Firewall Deny Bursts with IDS Signatures.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.69' running on index=netfw pan:traffic; index=ids sourcetype="suricata:alert" or pan:threat, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757) (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=netfw sourcetype="pan:traffic" action=deny earliest=-1h
| stats count by src, dest
| where count>500
| join type=left max=0 src [
    search index=ids (sourcetype="suricata:alert" OR sourcetype="pan:threat") earliest=-1h
    | rename src_ip as src
    | stats dc(signature) as sigs by src
  ]
| where isnotnull(sigs) AND sigs>0
| table src, dest, count, sigs
description: Joins short-window firewall denies with IDS alerts touching the same `src_ip` so automated reviews surface coordinated attack patterns without waiting for manual cross-index pivoting.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ids, netfw and sourcetypes pan:threat, pan:traffic, suricata:alert. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Tune `map` limits for performance; consider `join` with subsearch capped. (2) Replace with ES notable linking if preferred. (3) Document tuning for false positive rates. (4) Daily automated report to SOC leads. Indexes required: `index=ids`, `index=netfw`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=pan:threat`, `sourcetype=pan:traffic`, `sourcetype=suricata:alert`.
visualization: Table (correlated src), bubble chart (count vs sigs), timeline.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Change (CIM data model), All_Changes dataset.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `netfw`, `ids` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `11.4.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 11.4.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.69: Automated Log Review — Correlation of Firewall Deny Bursts with IDS Signatures." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `netfw`, `ids` under `pan:traffic`, `suricata:alert`, `pan:threat`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Tune `map` limits for performance; consider `join` with subsearch capped. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 2:** Replace with ES notable linking if preferred. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Document tuning for false positive rates. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Daily automated report to SOC leads. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `count > 500`. For "Automated Log Review — Correlation of Firewall Deny Bursts with IDS Signatures," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the PCI DSS control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `500` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by count
| stats avg(count) as mean, perc50(count) as p50, perc90(count) as p90, perc99(count) as p99, max(count) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `500` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `dc()` aggregation over the last hour is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.69: Automated Log Review — Correlation of Firewall Deny Bursts w]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -1h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.69,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=netfw sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `count`, `dest`, `sigs`, `src`. Run:

```spl
index=netfw sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table count, dest, sigs, src
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `count`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(count) as p50 perc90(count) as p90 perc99(count) as p99 max(count) as hi
```

The current threshold `500` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.69: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`correlated src`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Bubble Chart (`count vs sigs`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We joins short-window firewall denies with IDS alerts touching the same `src_ip` so automated reviews surface coordinated attack patterns without waiting for manual cross-index pivoting. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Automated Log Review — Correlation of Firewall Deny Bursts w' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper PCI DSS control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the PCI DSS control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with PCI DSS control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the PCI DSS programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
